items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: What is the minimum correlation typically accepted for adequate reliability?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: +0.80. “+0.50” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Generalising results from a lab to a real-life setting concerns which validity?
items.why: Correct answer: Ecological. “Internal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The BPS code is built on how many core ethical principles?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
